question: [File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: systemEntity()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate the `hiero.mirror.common.shard` or `hiero.mirror.common.realm` configuration properties (e.g., via environment variable injection or externalized config) to cause `SystemEntity.stakingRewardAccount()` to resolve to a wrong entity ID, so that `EntityStakeCalculatorImpl` anchors all pending reward calculations to an attacker-controlled account instead of 0.0.800, causing legitimate staking rewards to be miscalculated and effectively stolen?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: systemEntity()] Can an unprivileged external user submit crafted configuration input that sets `realm` or `shard` to a non-zero value before `@PostConstruct` runs on `CommonProperties`, causing `toEntityId(800L)` to produce an `EntityId` whose `getId()` matches an attacker-owned account, so that `EntityStakeCalculatorImpl.calculate()` calls `entityStakeRepository.updated(attackerAccountId)` and `getEndStakePeriod(attackerAccountId)`, redirecting the entire pending reward update cycle away from the real staking reward account?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: systemEntity()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the mutable `@Data`-generated setters on `CommonProperties` (e.g., `setShard()`, `setRealm()`) via an exposed Spring actuator endpoint or configuration refresh mechanism to change the singleton `INSTANCE` values at runtime, causing `SystemEntity.stakingRewardAccount()` to return a different `EntityId` mid-calculation and corrupting the `pending_reward` values stored in `entity_stake` for all staking accounts?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: hibernatePropertiesCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user bypass the `hibernate.criteria.literal_handling_mode = BIND` setting by crafting a request that reaches a Criteria API query path where user-controlled data is embedded as a literal rather than a bind parameter, allowing SQL injection into the `entity_stake` table to inflate their own `pending_reward` value and steal rewards from the staking reward account 0.0.800?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: hibernatePropertiesCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where the `HibernatePropertiesCustomizer` bean is not applied (e.g., by registering a competing `HibernatePropertiesCustomizer` bean that overrides `literal_handling_mode`), removing the BIND protection and enabling SQL injection through Criteria API queries that read or write `pending_reward` in `entity_stake`?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: hibernatePropertiesCustomizer()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `CustomJsonFormatMapper` registered via `AvailableSettings.JSON_FORMAT_MAPPER` to perform a deserialization attack on JSON-typed Hibernate columns, injecting malicious payloads that alter `pendingReward` or `stakeTotalStart` fields in `AbstractEntityStake` during a read-modify-write cycle?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: dataSource()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a crafted `JdbcConnectionDetails` bean (via a classpath-level override or Spring Boot auto-configuration ordering exploit) to redirect the `dataSource()` to an attacker-controlled database, causing the mirror node to read fraudulent `node_stake` reward rates and write inflated `pending_reward` values back to the legitimate database?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: dataSource()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `ObjectProvider<JdbcConnectionDetails> detailsProvider` resolution to supply a malicious `JdbcConnectionDetails` implementation that returns a JDBC URL pointing to a rogue database, so that `databaseWaiter.waitForDatabase()` succeeds against the rogue DB and the `HikariDataSource` is configured to connect there, allowing the attacker to serve fake `entity_stake` and `node_stake` data that inflates their `pending_reward`?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: dataSource()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the `@Lazy` annotation on `dataSource()` to create a race condition window between application startup and first datasource use, injecting a competing `DataSource` bean that satisfies `@ConditionalOnMissingBean(DataSource.class)` before the legitimate one is created, causing all staking reward DB writes to go to an attacker-controlled store?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: dataSource()] Can an unprivileged external user manipulate the `spring.datasource.hikari` configuration properties (scanned via `@ConfigurationProperties`) to set a malicious `connectionInitSql` or `connectionTestQuery` that executes arbitrary SQL on every new connection, modifying `pending_reward` values in `entity_stake` to redirect staking rewards to an attacker-controlled account?

[File: 'File Name: common/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/common/CommonConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: hikariConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user inject properties under `spring.datasource.hikari` (e.g., via environment variables or a mounted config file) to set `maximumPoolSize` to 0 or `connectionTimeout` to 0, causing the `HikariDataSource` to fail all connections and preventing the `EntityStakeCalculatorImpl` from persisting `pending_reward` updates, effectively freezing reward accrual and enabling a denial-of-reward attack?
